title: Strategic Account Plan
description: Create a comprehensive strategic account plan for a key account, including stakeholder mapping, competitive analysis, growth opportunities, risk assessment, and a 90-day action plan with specific milestones.
whyItMatters: Strategic account planning is what separates account managers from strategic account leaders. A great account plan turns reactive firefighting into proactive growth. It forces you to think beyond the next renewal and consider the long-term partnership: where can you expand, who else needs to be involved, what's the competitive threat, and what does the 3-year revenue trajectory look like?

steps.title: Account Overview and Research
steps.description: Choose a company (real or fictional) and create a comprehensive account profile. Document: company overview, industry, revenue, headcount, strategic priorities, recent news, executive team, competitive landscape, and technology stack. Include financial health indicators.
steps.tips: Use public sources: annual reports, LinkedIn, press releases, industry reports. For Australian companies, check ASX announcements, ASIC filings, and industry bodies.

steps.title: Stakeholder Map
steps.description: Map every relevant stakeholder: title, role in buying decisions (champion, decision-maker, influencer, blocker, user), relationship strength (1-5), what they care about, and your engagement plan. Identify at least 8 stakeholders across multiple teams and seniority levels.
steps.tips: Use a visual format — a diagram showing reporting lines and influence relationships. Colour-code by relationship strength. The biggest gaps in your map are your biggest risks.

steps.title: Current Footprint and Health Assessment
steps.description: Document your current presence in the account: products in use, contract values, renewal dates, satisfaction scores, support tickets, usage data, and any outstanding issues. Rate the overall health of the relationship (Red/Amber/Green) with specific evidence.
steps.tips: Be honest about red flags. An account where you have a great relationship with one champion but no executive sponsorship is more at risk than it appears.

steps.title: Competitive and Risk Analysis
steps.description: Document competitive threats: what other vendors are present, where they're strong, what's at risk of displacement. Assess internal risks: champion departure, budget cuts, reorganisation, technology shifts. For each risk, write a mitigation plan.
steps.tips: The most dangerous competitor is often a new executive who brings their preferred vendor from their previous company. Monitor leadership changes closely.

steps.title: Growth Strategy and 90-Day Plan
steps.description: Identify 3-5 specific growth opportunities: new teams, new use cases, product upgrades, additional seats. For each, estimate revenue, probability, champion, and timeline. Then create a 90-day action plan with specific weekly milestones: meetings to book, proposals to send, QBRs to schedule, relationships to build.
steps.tips: The best account plans have a 'protect, grow, deepen' structure. Protect existing revenue (renewals, risk mitigation). Grow through expansion (upsell, cross-sell). Deepen relationships (new stakeholders, executive alignment).

deliverables: A comprehensive account overview document (2-3 pages)
A visual stakeholder map with 8+ contacts, relationship scores, and engagement plans
A current footprint analysis with health assessment
A competitive and risk analysis with mitigation plans
A growth strategy with 3-5 prioritised opportunities and revenue estimates
A 90-day action plan with weekly milestones

rubric.description: The plan demonstrates genuine strategic thinking — not just a list of contacts and products, but a clear analysis of where the account is heading and how to align.
The stakeholder map is comprehensive (8+ contacts), multi-threaded (across teams and seniority levels), and includes honest relationship strength assessments.
Expansion opportunities are specific, sized, and tied to champions and trigger events. Revenue estimates are grounded in reality, not wishful thinking.
The 90-day plan has specific, time-bound actions with owners. It could be reviewed weekly to track progress. It's a working document, not a shelf document.
